panel.origin: The number of strokes an expert golfer is expected to need to complete a hole (or the course). The baseline every score is measured against.
panel.mixology: She hit the par-4 green in two and two-putted for a routine par — exactly the score the hole was designed to yield.
panel.examples: From the Latin 'par' (equal), borrowed via the stock-market sense of 'par value' (the standard or face value) into golf around 1900.

panel.origin: A score of one stroke under par on a hole. The bread-and-butter good score for skilled players.
panel.mixology: He drained a 15-foot putt for birdie, pumping his fist as the ball dropped one under par.
panel.examples: From early-1900s American slang where 'bird' meant anything excellent. An 'eagle' and 'albatross' later extended the bird theme to bigger feats.

panel.origin: A score of two strokes under par on a hole — a rare and exciting result, usually on a par-5 reached in two.
panel.mixology: She reached the par-5 in two and rolled in the putt for eagle, vaulting up the leaderboard.
panel.examples: An extension of the 'birdie' bird theme: if one-under is a birdie, two-under is a bigger bird — an eagle.

panel.origin: A score of one stroke over par on a hole. The most common 'mistake' score for average and good players alike.
panel.mixology: He missed the green and couldn't get up and down, settling for a bogey to drop one back.
panel.examples: From 'the Bogey Man' of a popular 1890s song; originally 'bogey' meant the score a steady, mythical opponent ('Colonel Bogey') would make — close to today's par.

panel.origin: A score of three strokes under par on a hole — also called a 'double eagle.' One of the rarest feats in golf.
panel.mixology: His second shot on the par-5 rolled into the cup for an albatross — a once-in-a-lifetime three-under.
panel.examples: Continuing the bird theme to its extreme: an albatross is a far larger, rarer bird than an eagle, fitting golf's rarest common score.

panel.origin: A numerical measure of a golfer's ability that lets players of different skill levels compete fairly by adjusting their scores.
panel.mixology: With a 12 handicap, she gets to subtract strokes, letting her match against a scratch golfer come down to the wire.
panel.examples: From the old game 'hand-in-cap', a betting practice where stakes were drawn from a cap to even the odds — the root of 'handicap' across all sports.

panel.origin: A hole-in-one — sinking the ball with a single stroke from the tee. Golf's most celebrated stroke of luck and skill.
panel.mixology: On the par-3 17th he made an ace, and tradition obliged him to buy drinks for the whole clubhouse afterward.
panel.examples: From the playing-card 'ace' (the single, highest card) via its general sense of 'the best' — a one-stroke hole being the ultimate.

panel.origin: Getting the ball into the hole in two strokes from off the green — typically a chip or pitch followed by one putt.
panel.mixology: He missed the green but got up and down from the bunker, his sand shot to three feet saving par.
panel.examples: Descriptive of the two-shot sequence: the ball goes 'up' (the approach onto the green) and 'down' (the putt into the hole).

panel.origin: A long shot played from the tee, usually with a driver, to start a hole and cover maximum distance.
panel.mixology: His drive split the fairway 300 yards out, leaving just a short iron into the green.
panel.examples: From the general sense of 'to drive' (to propel forcefully). The tee shot 'drives' the ball the farthest of any stroke in the bag.

panel.origin: A short, low shot played near the green that spends little time in the air and rolls toward the hole like a putt.
panel.mixology: She chipped it cleanly off the tight lie, the ball checking once and trickling to a foot from the cup.
panel.examples: From the everyday sense of a small, quick action — a 'chip' is a brief, clipped strike, in contrast to a full swing.

panel.origin: A stroke played on the green with a putter to roll the ball into (or near) the hole. The game's most precise shot.
panel.mixology: He left himself a tricky downhill putt and lagged it close, tapping in for his par.
panel.examples: From an old Scottish form of 'put' (to push or place gently). Golf preserves the original Scots spelling and pronunciation ('putt').

panel.origin: A short, lofted shot that flies higher than a chip and stops more quickly, used to approach the green from close range.
panel.mixology: From 60 yards she hit a soft pitch that landed by the pin and spun to a stop.
panel.examples: From 'to pitch' (to throw or toss). The shot 'pitches' the ball up into the air on a steep, lofted trajectory.

panel.origin: Two intentional curves: a 'fade' curves gently left-to-right (for a right-hander); a 'draw' curves right-to-left. Controlled shot shapes, not mistakes.
panel.mixology: He played a controlled fade around the trees, the ball drifting gently back toward the fairway.
panel.examples: 'Fade' from the sense of gradually drifting; 'draw' from the ball being 'drawn' back across its line. Their exaggerated, accidental cousins are the slice and hook.

panel.origin: Two unwanted curves: a 'slice' veers sharply away (left-to-right for a right-hander); a 'hook' veers sharply the other way. The amateur's common miss.
panel.mixology: His slice sent the ball curving into the trees, the clubface open at impact carving it hard right.
panel.examples: 'Slice' from the cutting, glancing contact that imparts the spin; 'hook' from the sharply curving, hook-like flight path.

panel.origin: A very high, soft shot hit with a wide-open clubface that lands almost vertically and stops fast — used to clear a hazard with little green to work with.
panel.mixology: Short-sided with the bunker between him and the pin, he opened the face and hit a delicate flop that landed soft.
panel.examples: From the way the ball 'flops' down almost straight onto the green with minimal roll — a dramatic, risky shot popularized by short-game artists.

panel.origin: A low, controlled shot hit with a shortened swing to keep the ball under wind or tree branches.
panel.mixology: Stuck under a low limb, she punched a knockdown shot that stayed low and ran up onto the green.
panel.examples: From 'to punch' (a short, sharp strike). The abbreviated, jabbing swing 'punches' the ball out low and controlled.

panel.origin: The closely-mown strip of grass between tee and green that offers the ideal lie for the next shot.
panel.mixology: Hitting the fairway off the tee is half the battle — from the short grass, every approach is easier.
panel.examples: From the nautical 'fairway' (a clear, navigable channel). Golf borrowed it for the clear, well-kept path toward the green.

panel.origin: The longer, thicker grass bordering the fairway that makes shots harder to control. Punishment for a wayward drive.
panel.mixology: His drive drifted into the thick rough, the long grass grabbing the clubhead and killing his distance.
panel.examples: From the plain sense of 'rough' (uneven, unkempt ground) — the deliberately untamed grass that contrasts with the manicured fairway.

panel.origin: The smooth, very closely-mown area surrounding the hole, designed for putting. Also called the 'putting green'.
panel.mixology: She hit the green in regulation and two-putted, her ball never leaving the short grass.
panel.examples: From the simple description of the well-watered, vivid-green putting surface — the most carefully maintained turf on the course.

panel.origin: A hazard consisting of a depression filled with sand, requiring a specialized sand shot to escape. Often called a 'sand trap'.
panel.mixology: He found the greenside bunker but splashed out beautifully, the ball floating up on a cushion of sand.
panel.examples: From the Scots 'bunker' (a seat, chest, or earthen bank). On Scotland's links courses, natural sandy hollows became the original golf bunkers.

panel.origin: The starting area of each hole, where players hit their first shot, usually from a small peg (the 'tee').
panel.mixology: Standing on the tee box, she teed it up high and ripped her opening drive down the middle.
panel.examples: From the Scots 'tee', the original small pile of sand (later a wooden peg) the ball was placed on to start a hole.

panel.origin: The flagstick marking the hole's location on the green, so players can see the target from a distance. Also called the 'flag'.
panel.mixology: He fired straight at the pin, the ball settling six feet from the hole for a birdie look.
panel.examples: From the simple sense of a slender pin or rod. The flag atop it lets golfers aim at a hole they can't otherwise see from the fairway.

panel.origin: A hole that bends left or right partway down its length, like a dog's hind leg, requiring a shaped or strategically placed drive.
panel.mixology: The par-4 doglegs sharply right, tempting big hitters to cut the corner over the trees.
panel.examples: From the resemblance of the angled fairway to the bent hind leg of a dog — a vivid, century-old golf coinage.

panel.origin: The slightly longer grass ringing the green, between the putting surface and the rough. Also called the 'apron' or 'collar'.
panel.mixology: Her ball came to rest on the fringe, and she chose to putt from just off the green's edge.
panel.examples: From the everyday 'fringe' (a border or edge). It's the transitional collar of grass framing the smooth green.

panel.origin: The longest-hitting club, with the largest head and lowest loft, used mainly off the tee for maximum distance. Also called the '1-wood'.
panel.mixology: He pulled the driver on the long par-5, swinging for the fences to reach the green in two.
panel.examples: Named for its job: it 'drives' the ball the farthest. Modern oversized titanium drivers are a far cry from the wooden originals.

panel.origin: A high-lofted iron designed for short, high shots and getting out of trouble — pitching, sand, lob, and gap wedges each serve a range.
panel.mixology: From 90 yards she reached for a wedge, lofting the ball high so it landed soft and stopped quickly.
panel.examples: From the wedge-like shape and steep loft of the clubface, which lifts the ball sharply upward — the most lofted clubs in the bag.

panel.origin: A numbered set of clubs (typically 3 through 9) with flatter, angled faces, used for a wide range of approach shots. Lower numbers hit farther.
panel.mixology: He flushed a 7-iron to the heart of the green, the crisp contact sending it right at the flag.
panel.examples: From the iron (now steel) clubheads that distinguished them from the old wooden-headed 'woods'. The numbering reflects increasing loft.

panel.origin: A flat-faced club designed solely for rolling the ball along the green into the hole. The most-used club in any round.
panel.mixology: She lined up the putt, brought the putter back smoothly, and rolled it dead center.
panel.examples: Named directly for its single job — to 'putt'. It's the one club a golfer might use on every single hole of a round.

panel.origin: A club blending the features of an iron and a fairway wood — easier to hit than a long iron, with a rounded head. Also called a 'rescue' club.
panel.mixology: Replacing his hard-to-hit 3-iron with a hybrid, he found long approaches suddenly far more forgiving.
panel.examples: Named literally for being a 'hybrid' of two club types. The popular brand name 'Rescue' captures its job — bailing players out of long shots.

panel.origin: The angle of a clubface relative to vertical, which determines how high and far the ball flies. More loft means higher, shorter shots.
panel.mixology: The lob wedge's huge loft sent the ball nearly straight up, dropping it softly over the bunker.
panel.examples: From the old sense of 'loft' (a height or upper space) — the angle that 'lofts' the ball into the air. Each club's loft is carefully calibrated.

panel.origin: An informal 'do-over' — replaying a bad shot with no penalty. Not allowed under the rules, but common in casual rounds.
panel.mixology: After a snap-hook off the first tee, his buddies waved him to take a mulligan and try again.
panel.examples: Popularly traced to a 1920s golfer named David Mulligan, who supposedly took an extra shot — though several origin stories compete and none is certain.

panel.origin: A short putt that the other players agree to count as made without actually being struck — conceded as too easy to miss.
panel.mixology: His ball stopped six inches away, and his opponent waved it off as a gimme.
panel.examples: From the casual contraction of 'give me' (the stroke). Strictly, gimmes aren't allowed in stroke play, but they're a friendly staple of casual golf.

panel.origin: A warning shouted to alert other players that a ball is heading their way and they should take cover.
panel.mixology: His drive sailed toward the next fairway and he yelled 'Fore!' as loud as he could.
panel.examples: Likely from the military 'beware before!' or a caddie's old cry of 'forecaddie' — a warning to those ahead. The exact origin is debated.

panel.origin: A golfer with a handicap of zero, skilled enough to play to par on average. The benchmark of an excellent amateur.
panel.mixology: As a scratch golfer, she gets no handicap strokes — she's expected to shoot around par every time out.
panel.examples: From the old sporting sense of 'scratch' as the starting line from which the best competitors begin, receiving no head start or allowance.

panel.origin: A horribly mis-hit shot struck off the club's heel (hosel), sending the ball squirting sharply sideways. Golf's most dreaded miss.
panel.mixology: He shanked it dead right off the hosel, the ball rocketing sideways into the trees — every golfer's nightmare.
panel.examples: From the 'shank' (the neck or hosel of the club) that strikes the ball. So feared that some golfers refuse to even say the word aloud.

panel.origin: Affectionate slang for the clubhouse bar — the 'hole' players head to after finishing the 18 on the course.
panel.mixology: After a rough round, the foursome consoled themselves at the 19th hole, swapping stories over drinks.
panel.examples: A playful coinage: a standard course has 18 holes, so the bar where golfers gather afterward becomes the unofficial '19th'.
